content.full_text:
Memorandum 
April 6, 1983 
TO: 
Advice Request Meeting Participants 
FROM: 
Janis Shank McLean 
RE: 
Reporting of Gifts (Emphasis on Tickets) 
Under the Act, a filer is only required to report his or her 
community property interest in the income of a spouse. 
The term 
"income" includes gifts. 
(Government Code Section 82030(a». 
California law specifies that gifts received by a spouse during 
marriage are the separate property of the spouse (Cal. Const. 
Art. I, Sec. 21; Civil Code Section 5107) 
Thus, from 1976-1978, 
the Commission did not require filers to report gifts received 
by a spouse on their Statements of Economic Interests. 
Similarly, filers were not required to report gifts received by 
his or her dependent children. 
(See Cory, No. 75-94-A, 2 FPPC 
48, April 22, 1976.) 
The following are summaries of the 1976-1978 materials that 
I found discussing the reporting of gifts (I searched the 
computer under Sections 82028, 87207 (refined with the word 
"g if t If) and 18727): 
M-78-3l6 
A legislator who receives eight tickets to 
Disneyland and four free admissions to a dinner and a movie 
premier must report all of them because none of the other 
tickets or admissions were specifically given to the persons who 
used them. 
77-493 
A legislator who asks a race track to provide eight 
tickets to his constituents must report the tickets as gifts if 
he exercises direction and control over the tickets (i.e., if 
the tickets are placed at his disposal, or if there is a pattern 
of the legislator frequently asking for tickets and the track 
providing them). 
The tickets are not reportable if he doesn't 
exercise control over them (i.e., if, on rare occasions, he 
merely asks a friend at the track to do a favor for the 
constituents) • 
77-271 
The cost of food, transportation and lodging for an 
official's spouse, paid by a private entity as part of a 
speaking engagement, is not reportable. 
This is consistent with 
the Cory Opinion (see below) . 


Advice Request Meeting Participants 
Apr i 1 6, 198 3 
Page 2 
77-492 
Gifts of meals and vacations are reported as joint 
gifts to the husband and wife. 
However, only the filer's share 
is counted and the amount of the gift must be double the 
reporting threshold to be reportable. 
The official's share may 
be reported as a separate gift. 
78-259 
Only the filer's share of a wedding gift is counted 
and the value of the gift must be double the reporting 
threshold, (See the Tor res Opinion, below). 
76-481 
A gift was made to a filer's children when they were 
allowed to move from their coach seat to first class to be with 
the official. 
The filer has no reporting obligation. 
Cory Opinion, A-75-094 
Gifts received by a spouse are 
separate property and are not reportable by the filer. 
Gifts to 
dependent children are also not reportable. 
However, a gift 
ostensibly made to the spouse or dependent child will be 
considered a gift to the official if: 
1. 
The nature of the gift is such that the official is 
likely to enjoy direct benefit or use of the gift to at least 
the same extent as the ostensible donee; and 
2. 
The official in fact enjoys such direct benefit or use; 
and 
3. 
There are no additional circumstances negating an intent 
to make an indirect gift to the official. 
Under this standard, meals and trips are bona fide gifts to 
a child and aren't reportable. 
Torres Opinion, 74-163 
Wedding presents are joint gifts, 
unless peculiarly adaptable to the personal use of one spouse, 
and only the filer's share of the gift is counted toward meeting 
the threshold amount. 
Hopkins Opinion, 77-022 
In this Opinion, all of the tickets 
received were reportable by the filer. 
(However, none of the 
examples stated that a ticket was identified as being for a 
spouse or child. 
The filer appeared to exercise complete 
direction and control over the use of the tickets) . 
Beginning sometime after 1978, a totally separate rule 
volved for reporting gifts of tickets. 
Under the new rule, all 
tickets received have to be reported, even if a ticket appears 
to be intended for a spouse or child. 
The rationale for this is 


Advice Request Meeting Participants 
April 6, 1983 
Page 3 
an assumption that the spouse or child only receives the ticket 
because of their relationship to the filer. 
This separate reporting rule for tickets is neither 
justified, nor desireable. 
It does not seem to have any support 
in the Act and, in fact, directly contradicts the "community 
property" limitation in Section 82030, and numerous Commission 
opinions and advice letters. 
It is inconsistent because it can 
be asserted that almost all gifts received by spouses and 
children from sources other than family members or personal 
friends are received because of a recipient's relationship to a 
filer and, thuSi the rule should logically be extended to othe~ 
types of gifts._1 Lastly, a separate rule creates a reporting 
trap for filers. Anyone reading Cory would assume that a ticket 
intended for, and used by, a spouse would not be reportable, yet 
it is.£1 
In order to rectify this situation, gifts of tickets bearing 
a spouse's or child's name, or given under circumstances 
indicating that the tickets are intended specifically for a 
spouse or child ,11 should not be reportable. 
However, if the 
circumstances indicate that the filer exercised direction or 
control over the tickets (for example, by giving them to someone 
else) I 
then they should be reported. 
JSM: km 
~I This rule cannot be extended without amending Section 
82030 to eliminate the "community property" rule and abandoning 
our past advice. 
~I It also creates incongruous results. 
For instance, if 
a filer and his spouse receive theater tickets and a dinner, 
both of the tickets are reportable, but only half of the dinner. 
~I Such as where the envelope said, "Assemblyman Jones 
and Family."

sections.question_synthetic: Should gifts of tickets received by a filer's spouse or dependent children be reportable under California's Political Reform Act, and how should the FPPC's ticket reporting rule be reconciled with prior community property opinions?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Gifts of tickets bearing a spouse's or child's name, or given under circumstances indicating they are intended specifically for a spouse or child, should not be reportable. However, if circumstances indicate the filer exercised direction or control over the tickets, they should be reported. This approach aligns with existing community property limitations and past Commission opinions rather than the current separate rule for all tickets.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an internal FPPC memorandum to advice request meeting participants rather than a traditional letter responding to a specific request. No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections are present; content is organized as analysis and recommendations. Document discusses historical evolution of gift reporting rules from 1976-1983.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should gifts of tickets received by a filer's spouse or dependent children be reportable under California's Political Reform Act, and how should the FPPC's ticket reporting rule be reconciled with prior community property opinions?

CONCLUSION: Gifts of tickets bearing a spouse's or child's name, or given under circumstances indicating they are intended specifically for a spouse or child, should not be reportable. However, if circumstances indicate the filer exercised direction or control over the tickets, they should be reported. This approach aligns with existing community property limitations and past Commission opinions rather than the current separate rule for all tickets.

embedding.summary: This memorandum addresses the reporting of gifts, particularly tickets, under the Political Reform Act. It critiques a post-1978 rule requiring all tickets to be reported regardless of intended recipient, arguing it contradicts the community property limitation in Government Code Section 82030 and prior FPPC opinions, and recommends that tickets clearly intended for spouses or children should be exempt from reporting unless the filer exercised control over them.
